nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical coral reefs and lagoons with hard substrate and light sand covering; found on reef slopes, lagoon floors, and rocky outcroppings from the surface to moderate depths

decorPreferences: porous live rock for foot attachment
varied substrate with sandy and rocky areas
open rockwork to allow disc expansion
powerhead guards — anemone will wander and can be shredded by impellers
no sharp surfaces that can damage the column

diet.notes: The Sebae Anemone draws most of its energy from symbiotic zooxanthellae — photosynthetic algae living within its tissues — and supplements this with active predation. Target-feed weekly with silversides, krill, small pieces of shrimp, clams, marine worms, or other meaty marine foods. A hosting clownfish can provide additional nutrients through waste and mucus exchange. Avoid overfeeding — uneaten food left in the tentacles degrades water quality, and bloating can be fatal.

goodWith: most other Amphiprion species (14+ documented host relationships)
reef-safe fish that will not nip tentacles
avoidWith: other anemone species — territorial and will sting neighboring anemones
SPS and LPS corals without adequate separation — potent sting damages neighbors
powerheads and circulation pumps without foam guards
angelfish and butterflyfish — tend to nip tentacles
triggers, puffers, and other large predatory fish

summary: The Sebae Anemone (Heteractis crispa) is a large, mobile host anemone widely documented to associate with 14 or more clownfish species in the wild. It is generally considered an advanced species, requiring high-intensity lighting, stable reef water chemistry, and careful aquascaping to manage its wandering behavior and potent sting.
careNotes:
The Sebae Anemone, also sold under the name Leathery Sea Anemone (Heteractis crispa), is generally considered an advanced-care species in the marine hobby. It is notable for hosting a wide range of clownfish — 14 to 15 Amphiprion and Premnas species have been documented as hosts in the wild — making it one of the most ecologically significant host anemones and a popular choice for dedicated clownfish keepers.

Taxonomic note: In 2023, this species was moved out of the genus Heteractis and reclassified as Radianthus crispa. Both names appear in current hobby literature and retailer listings, though Heteractis crispa remains more widely used in aquarium trade and care guides. The closely related Ritteri Anemone underwent a parallel reclassification from Heteractis magnifica to Radianthus magnifica. See [ritteri-anemone] for comparison.

Key care requirements:

• Lighting: High-intensity lighting is generally required — typically recommended in the range of 200–400 PAR. The anemone tends to wander toward light sources and may climb high in the tank seeking greater illumination. New arrivals should be acclimated gradually over several weeks, starting at lower intensities to prevent bleaching.

• Wandering behavior: Sebae Anemones are known to roam extensively, particularly in tanks with inadequate lighting. All powerheads and circulation pumps must be covered with foam guards to prevent the anemone from being drawn into impellers. An open aquascape with varied rocky and sandy zones is recommended to allow natural foot placement.

• Foot attachment: The foot anchors to live rock or buried hard substrate, often settling into crevices or under rock overhangs. Sandy substrate can be used for burrowing attachment but is not sufficient on its own. Porous live rock tends to be preferred over smooth surfaces.

• Flow: Moderate, intermittent flow is generally recommended. Strong direct laminar flow can stress the anemone; alternating gyre-type or wave-making flow tends to work better. Tentacles should gently sway but not stream flat.

• Water quality: Stable, pristine water chemistry is essential. Nitrates should generally be kept below 10–20 ppm and phosphates below 0.05–0.1 ppm, with ammonia and nitrite at zero. Iodine and trace element supplementation has been reported to support long-term health.

• Clownfish symbiosis: The presence of a compatible, actively hosting clownfish — particularly A. clarkii, A. sebae, or A. polymnus, its primary wild hosts — has been reported to improve acclimation and long-term health. A healthy, well-settled anemone becomes noticeably sticky and grips firmly to objects placed near it.

• Feeding: Target-feed weekly with small pieces of meaty marine foods. Spot-feeding with tongs or a turkey baster near the oral disc tends to produce the best results. Do not overfeed — uneaten prey left in the tentacles can degrade water quality quickly.

• Acclimation difficulty: Newly imported wild-caught specimens are often bleached (meaning their symbiotic zooxanthellae algae have been expelled or depleted) and may require weeks to months of careful feeding and gradual light acclimation to recover color and health. Captive-acclimated or tank-raised individuals are generally significantly easier to establish.

• Size in captivity: The oral disc typically reaches 20–50 cm in well-maintained aquaria, with wild specimens occasionally exceeding this range. Individual body size is influenced by tank volume and lighting quality.

• Comparison to Ritteri Anemone (H. magnifica): H. crispa is generally considered somewhat easier to keep than the Ritteri, with a more flexible foot-placement habit — it attaches to rock, branching coral, or semi-buried substrate rather than exclusively seeking exposed high-light positions — and is reported to tolerate moderate conditions somewhat better. Both species carry potent stings and require high-intensity lighting.
breedingNotes: Captive sexual reproduction has not been documented in the hobby. In the wild, H. crispa reproduces both sexually — by releasing eggs and sperm directly into the water column — and asexually via scissiparity, a process in which the body column splits lengthwise to produce two separate individuals. This form of asexual division has been documented in captive specimens kept under stable, long-term conditions.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Sebae Anemone
primary.notes: Temperature 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, min tank 30 gal (113L), max size 20 in (50cm), care level Difficult, carnivore, temperament Aggressive, reef compatible with caution, lighting High, flow Medium, weekly feeding.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Heteractis crispa (Radianthus crispa)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family Stichodactylidae, Order Actiniaria), 2023 reclassification to Radianthus crispa, native range (Red Sea/East Africa to Polynesia, south Japan to Australia/New Caledonia), oral disc 20–50 cm, tentacle length 10–15 cm, depth 0–40 m, hosts 15 anemonefish species, scissiparity reproduction.

additional.name: Reeflings — Heteractis crispa Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 22–26°C, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, min tank 200L, disc ~50cm, care 'moderate to easy', semi-aggressive. Significantly larger min tank (200L vs 113L); 200L adopted as the more conservative/appropriate value. Temperature lower bound agrees; SG minimum averaged between 1.020 and 1.023 → 1.022.
